title: श्री शिव तत्त्व स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
तत्त्वं शिवस्य परमं निर्गुणं निर्विकारकम्।
निराकारं निरालम्बं ज्ञानानन्दस्वरूपिणम्॥१॥

सर्वव्यापी सर्वसाक्षी सर्वात्मा सर्वकारणम्।
शिवतत्त्वं परं ब्रह्म प्रणमामि सनातनम्॥२॥

न तत्र सूर्यो भाति न चन्द्रतारकं
नेमा विद्युतो भान्ति कुतोऽयमग्निः।
तमेव भान्तमनुभाति सर्वं
तस्य भासा सर्वमिदं विभाति॥३॥

नित्यं शुद्धं निराभासं निरवद्यं निरञ्जनम्।
नित्यबोधस्वरूपं तं शिवतत्त्वं नमाम्यहम्॥४॥

न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचिन्
नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः।
अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो
न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे॥५॥

यतो वाचो निवर्तन्ते अप्राप्य मनसा सह।
तस्य शिवतत्त्वस्य आनन्दं ब्रह्मणो विदन्॥६॥

आत्मा त्वं गिरिजा मतिः सहचराः प्राणाः शरीरं गृहम्।
पूजा ते विषयोपभोगरचना निद्रा समाधिस्थितिः॥७॥

सञ्चारः पदयोः प्रदक्षिणविधिः स्तोत्राणि सर्वा गिरो।
यद्यत् कर्म करोमि तत्तदखिलं शम्भो तवाराधनम्॥८॥

शिवोऽहं शिवोऽहं शिवोऽहमेव केवलम्।
सच्चिदानन्दरूपोऽहं सदाशिवस्वरूपवान्॥९॥

शिवतत्त्वमिदं स्तोत्रं यः पठेत् शिवसन्निधौ।
शिवज्ञानमवाप्नोति शिवसायुज्यमाप्नुयात्॥

॥ इति श्री शिव तत्त्व स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
Tattvam Shivasya Paramam Nirgunam Nirvikarakam.
Nirakaram Niralambam Jnanananda-Svarupinam.||1||

Sarvavyapi Sarvasakshi Sarvatma Sarvakaranam.
Shivaatattvam Param Brahma Pranamami Sanatanam.||2||

Na Tatra Suryo Bhati Na Chandratara-kam
Nema Vidyuto Bhanti Kuto'yam Agnih.
Tameva Bhantam Anubhati Sarvam
Tasya Bhasa Sarvamidam Vibhati.||3||

Nityam Shuddham Nirabhasa-m Niravadyam Niranjanam.
Nityabodhasvarupam Tam Shivatattvam Namamyaham.||4||

Na Jayate Mriyate Va Kadachin
Nayam Bhutva Bhavita Va Na Bhuyah.
Ajo Nityah Shashvato'yam Purano
Na Hanyate Hanyamane Sharire.||5||

Yato Vacho Nivartante Aprapya Manasa Saha.
Tasya Shivatattvasya Anandam Brahmano Vidan.||6||

Atma Tvam Girija Matih Sahacharah Pranah Shariram Griham.
Puja Te Vishayopabhoga-Rachana Nidra Samadhi-Sthitih.||7||

Sancharah Padayoh Pradakshina-Vidhih Stotrani Sarva Giro.
Yadyat Karma Karomi Tattadakhilam Shambho Tavaradhanam.||8||

Shivo'ham Shivo'ham Shivo'ham Eva Kevalam.
Sachchidanandarupo'ham Sadashivasvarupavan.||9||

Shivatattvam Idam Stotram Yah Pathet Shivasannidhau.
Shivajnanam Avapnoti Shivasayujyam Apnuyat.

|| Iti Shri Shiva Tatva Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
शिव का तत्त्व परम है — निर्गुण, निर्विकार, निराकार, निरालम्ब, ज्ञानानन्द स्वरूप।

सर्वव्यापी, सर्वसाक्षी, सर्वात्मा, सर्वकारण — ऐसे परम ब्रह्म शिवतत्त्व सनातन को मैं प्रणाम करता हूँ।

वहाँ न सूर्य चमकता है, न चन्द्र-तारे, न विद्युत, अग्नि की तो बात ही क्या। उसी के प्रकाशित होने से सब प्रकाशित होते हैं, उसकी भासा से यह सब जगत विभासित होता है।

नित्य, शुद्ध, निराभास, निरवद्य, निरञ्जन, नित्यबोध स्वरूप शिवतत्त्व को मैं नमस्कार करता हूँ।

न जन्मता है, न मरता है, न होकर फिर होने वाला है। अजन्मा, नित्य, शाश्वत, पुराण — शरीर के नष्ट होने पर भी नष्ट नहीं होता।

जहाँ से वाणी मन सहित लौट आती है — उस शिवतत्त्व के आनन्द को ब्रह्मवेत्ता जानते हैं।

हे शिव! आप मेरी आत्मा हैं, गिरिजा मेरी बुद्धि, प्राण सहचर, शरीर गृह है। विषय-भोग आपकी पूजा है, निद्रा समाधि-स्थिति है।

पैरों का संचार प्रदक्षिणा है, सब वाणी स्तोत्र हैं। जो भी कर्म करता हूँ वह सब हे शम्भो! आपकी आराधना है।

मैं शिव हूँ, मैं शिव हूँ, मैं केवल शिव ही हूँ। सच्चिदानन्द स्वरूप, सदाशिव स्वरूपवान् हूँ।

जो शिव सन्निधि में इस शिवतत्त्व स्तोत्र का पाठ करता है, वह शिवज्ञान प्राप्त करता है और शिव-सायुज्य पाता है।

viniyog.shloka: अस्य श्रीशिवतत्त्वस्तोत्रमन्त्रस्य आदिशंकराचार्य ऋषिः। अनुष्टुप्-शार्दूलविक्रीडित छन्दः। श्रीशिवतत्त्व देवता। ॐ नमः शिवाय बीजम्। ज्ञानशक्तिः। शिवसायुज्यप्राप्तिः कीलकम्। मोक्षार्थे पाठे विनियोगः॥

benefits: Reveals the ultimate metaphysical truth of Shiva Tattva
Leads to Advaita realization — identity with Shiva
Traditionally recited for removal of ignorance and grants supreme knowledge
Transforms every action into worship of Shiva
Ideal for deep meditation and philosophical contemplation
description: Shiva Tatva Stotram (श्री शिव तत्त्व स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise dedicated to Lord Shiva. Attributed to Advaita Vedanta Tradition (Attributed to Adi Shankaracharya), this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Devotees recite this prayer to transform every action into worship of Shiva and reveal the ultimate metaphysical truth of Shiva Tattva. It is also said to grant Shiva Sayujya (union with Shiva). It is especially recommended on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri for maximum spiritual benefit. With 9 verses and a reading time of about 6 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Shiva Tatva Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shiva Tatva Stotram in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Philosophical hymn revealing the ultimate Shiva Tattva for self-realization and moksha.
source: Advaita Vedanta Tradition (Attributed to Adi Shankaracharya)
relatedSearches: Shiva tattva Sat Chit Ananda exposition
Suddha tattvas Shiva Shakti Sadashiva Ishvara Vidya

seoFaqs.question: Is there a single canonical Sanskrit text titled 'Shiva Tatva Stotram'?
seoFaqs.answer: There is no single universally cited Puranic or Agamic chapter titled 'Shiva Tatva Stotram' in the way that the Shiva Mahimna Stotra or Shiva Panchakshara Stotram are textually fixed. The title circulates in regional devotional usage to describe philosophical hymns that expound the Shaiva-tattva (essence of Shiva) doctrine. Closely allied texts include the Vedasara Shiva Stava attributed to Adi Shankaracharya, sections of the Shiva Mahimna Stotra that contemplate Shiva as the absolute, and chapters of Shaiva Siddhanta granthas such as Shiva-jnana-bodha by Meykandar that present the Shiva-tattva systematically. Devotees encountering this slug should treat it as a contemplative-philosophical hymn rather than a fixed-text recitation, and consult their lineage teacher for the specific recension followed locally.

seoFaqs.question: What does 'Shiva Tattva' actually mean in Shaiva philosophy?
seoFaqs.answer: Tattva is a Sanskrit word meaning 'thatness', 'principle' or 'reality', and Shiva-tattva refers to the foundational principle from which everything arises, in which everything is sustained, and into which everything dissolves. In Shaiva Siddhanta and Kashmir Shaiva schemes, the manifest universe is described through 36 tattvas, of which Shiva-tattva is the highest, beyond Shakti-tattva, Sadashiva-tattva and Ishvara-tattva. Para-Shiva, the unmanifest absolute, transcends even this tattva schema. A 'Shiva Tatva Stotram' in this sense is any hymn that contemplates Shiva as the unconditioned ground of being, beyond name, form, time and space, while paradoxically also being immanent in every manifest principle. This double aspect, transcendent and immanent, is the philosophical kernel of all Shiva-tattva expositions.

seoFaqs.question: Which classical hymn most closely matches the Shiva Tatva Stotram theme?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vedasara Shiva Stava attributed to Adi Shankaracharya is the closest match. It is a compact hymn in which each verse identifies Shiva with a different Vedantic principle, the unmanifest substratum, the Sat-Chit-Ananda absolute, the witness of all states, the source of the Vedas. Greenmesg.org reproduces the Sanskrit text with English meaning. Other allied texts include sections of the Shiva Mahimna Stotram (especially verses 24-26 that identify Shiva and Vishnu as one), and the philosophical opening verses of the Shivanandalahari. Devotees seeking a Shiva-tattva contemplative hymn often combine excerpts from these sources rather than relying on a single labelled text.

seoFaqs.question: What are the 36 tattvas referenced in Shiva-tattva contemplation?
seoFaqs.answer: The 36 tattvas of Shaiva ontology are arranged in three groups. The Shuddha (pure) tattvas are five: Shiva, Shakti, Sadashiva, Ishvara and Suddhavidya, said to be directly emanated by Shiva. The Shuddha-Ashuddha (pure-impure) tattvas are seven: Maya, Kala, Vidya, Raga, Kala (time), Niyati and Purusha. The Ashuddha (impure) tattvas are twenty-four: Prakriti, Buddhi, Ahamkara, Manas, the five jnanendriyas, the five karmendriyas, the five tanmatras and the five mahabhutas. Saivism.net and the Wikipedia entry on Tattva (Shaivism) preserve this enumeration. A Shiva-tattva contemplation moves the practitioner up this hierarchy from gross body experience to subtle elements, then to mind, then to the pure tattvas, ultimately resting in Shiva-tattva itself as the ground of all categories.

seoFaqs.question: How is a Shiva Tatva Stotram traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Because the text exists in regional rather than canonical form, recitation practice varies. Where a Vedasara Shiva Stava or similar philosophical hymn is followed, the standard prescription is daily morning recitation after bath, seated facing east or north before a Shiva lingam, with vibhuti applied. Recitation is slow and meaning-aware, since the purpose is contemplative reorientation rather than petitionary mantra-japa. Many sadhakas pair the recitation with a brief silent contemplation after each verse, allowing the philosophical content to settle. A typical session lasts 10 to 15 minutes. Devotees uncertain which recension to use should consult their lineage acharya, since regional Shaiva traditions in Kerala, Karnataka, Kashmir and Bengal each maintain slightly different curated texts under this broad title.

seoFaqs.question: What contemplative benefits do teachers attribute to Shiva-tattva recitation?
seoFaqs.answer: Shaiva teachers describe Shiva-tattva contemplation as the highest form of stotra-paath because it directly trains the mind in correct ontological understanding rather than petitioning for outcomes. Benefits cited include progressive dissolution of false identification with body, mind and ego (chitta-shuddhi); cultivation of viveka (discriminative awareness) between the changing and the unchanging; preparation for nididhyasana (Vedantic absorption) and Shaiva pratyabhijna (recognition); and gradual softening of attachment to worldly outcomes as the practitioner identifies more with Shiva-tattva and less with surface phenomena. Tradition cautions that these benefits accrue only to sustained daily practice combined with study under a competent teacher, since philosophical hymns can otherwise remain abstract recitations without producing the intended inner shift.
